one_liner: A 3-year-old male with no significant past medical history presents with 5 days of high fever, bilateral conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, and a diffuse polymorphous rash.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient's mother reports that he has had a persistent high fever (up to 40°C) for the past 5 days that has been minimally responsive to acetaminophen and ibuprofen. On day 2 of the fever, she noticed both of his eyes became red without any discharge. On day 3, his lips became cracked and red, and his tongue appeared swollen and bumpy. A diffuse red rash appeared on his trunk and extremities on day 4. Today she noticed his hands and feet appear swollen and red. He has been irritable and has had decreased oral intake. He has had no cough, runny nose, diarrhea, or vomiting. No recent travel or sick contacts. No recent immunizations.
stage2.pmh: No significant past medical history. Born full-term. Up to date on immunizations.
stage2.medications: Acetaminophen 160 mg (15 mg/kg) PO q4-6h PRN, Ibuprofen 100 mg (10 mg/kg) PO q6h PRN
stage2.family_history: No family history of autoimmune disease or cardiac disease. Japanese-American descent.
stage2.social_history: Lives with both parents. Attends preschool. No secondhand smoke exposure. No recent travel. No pets.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Irritable, febrile-appearing toddler who is difficult to console; does not appear toxic. HEENT: Bilateral bulbar conjunctival injection without exudate; periorbital edema absent; Lips are erythematous, cracked, and fissured; Tongue is erythematous with prominent fungiform papillae (strawberry tongue); Oropharyngeal mucosa is diffusely erythematous; TMs clear bilaterally. Neck: Single right anterior cervical lymph node measuring approximately 2 cm, firm, mildly tender, non-fluctuant. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; normal S1, S2. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; no hepatosplenomegaly. Extremities: Bilateral erythema and non-pitting edema of the dorsa of hands and feet; palms and soles are erythematous. Skin: Diffuse polymorphous maculopapular rash predominantly on the trunk and proximal extremities; involvement of the perineal region with erythema and early desquamation.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Leukocytosis with neutrophil predominance is common in the acute phase of this condition.
Mild normocytic anemia is typical of this condition due to inflammation-mediated suppression of erythropoiesis.
Thrombocytosis is a hallmark of this condition, typically peaking in the second to third week of illness; severe thrombocytosis increases coronary artery aneurysm risk.
Markedly elevated ESR reflects systemic inflammation characteristic of this condition; ESR may paradoxically rise after IVIG treatment.
Significantly elevated CRP is a marker of systemic inflammation; CRP >30 mg/L is one of the supplemental lab criteria for incomplete this condition.
Hypoalbuminemia is common in this condition due to vascular leak from vasculitis and is a supplemental lab criterion for incomplete KD.
Elevated transaminases occur in approximately 40% of KD patients and reflect hepatic involvement from vasculitis; ALT >50 is a supplemental criterion.

lab_results.result: WBC 12/hpf (sterile pyuria), no bacteria, no nitrites, trace protein
lab_results.clinical_significance: Sterile pyuria (WBC in urine with negative culture) is a characteristic finding in KD due to urethritis from vasculitis; ≥10 WBC/hpf is a supplemental criterion.
Elevated BNP can indicate myocardial involvement (myocarditis) in this condition, even in the absence of overt heart failure.

imaging_results.result: Normal left ventricular function (EF 62%). No coronary artery aneurysms identified. Left main coronary artery Z-score 1.8 (upper normal). Right coronary artery Z-score 1.5. No pericardial effusion. Mild mitral regurgitation. Normal aortic root dimensions.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Baseline echocardiogram is essential in suspected KD to assess coronary arteries; borderline coronary dilation (Z-score 1.5-2.5) warrants close follow-up. Mild mitral regurgitation can be seen in acute KD due to valvulitis.

imaging_results.result: Normal cardiac silhouette. Clear lung fields bilaterally. No pleural effusions. No infiltrates.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Normal CXR helps exclude pneumonia and cardiac enlargement; peribronchial cuffing may occasionally be seen in KD.

stage5.final_clue: On hospital day 2, the echocardiogram reveals a left main coronary artery Z-score that has increased to 2.3, confirming coronary artery involvement.

debrief.classic_presentation: Kawasaki disease classically presents in children under 5 years (peak 1-2 years) with fever lasting ≥5 days plus at least 4 of 5 principal features: (1) bilateral non-exudative conjunctival injection, (2) oral mucous membrane changes (strawberry tongue, lip cracking/erythema), (3) polymorphous rash, (4) extremity changes (erythema, edema, later periungual desquamation), and (5) cervical lymphadenopathy (≥1.5 cm, usually unilateral). Boys and children of East Asian descent are at higher risk.
debrief.pathophysiology: Kawasaki disease is a systemic medium-vessel vasculitis of unknown etiology, though an infectious trigger in genetically susceptible individuals is suspected. The vasculitis preferentially targets coronary arteries. The inflammatory cascade involves activation of the innate immune system with infiltration of neutrophils, CD8+ T cells, and IgA-secreting plasma cells into vessel walls. Pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-1, IL-6) drive endothelial damage, elastic lamina destruction, and myofibroblast proliferation. This leads to coronary artery aneurysm formation in 15-25% of untreated cases. The aneurysms may thrombose, causing myocardial infarction, or develop stenosis during remodeling.
debrief.treatment_overview: Standard treatment is high-dose IVIG (2 g/kg as a single infusion over 10-12 hours) plus high-dose aspirin (80-100 mg/kg/day divided QID) during the acute febrile phase. Once afebrile for 48-72 hours, aspirin is reduced to low-dose (3-5 mg/kg/day) and continued for 6-8 weeks until follow-up echocardiogram is normal. IVIG should ideally be given within the first 10 days of illness to reduce coronary aneurysm risk from 25% to <5%. For IVIG-refractory cases (persistent fever 36 hours after IVIG), a second dose of IVIG or infliximab (anti-TNF-alpha) may be used. Corticosteroids are considered adjunctive therapy in high-risk patients.
high_yield_pearls: Kawasaki disease is the LEADING cause of acquired heart disease in children in developed countries, surpassing rheumatic fever.
IVIG must be given within the first 10 days of illness to reduce the risk of coronary artery aneurysms from ~25% to <5%; earlier treatment is associated with better outcomes.

Incomplete Kawasaki disease (fever ≥5 days but only 2-3 classic features) should be suspected in any child with prolonged unexplained fever and elevated inflammatory markers — supplemental lab criteria (CRP ≥3 mg/dL, ESR ≥40, anemia, thrombocytosis after day 7, hypoalbuminemia, elevated ALT, sterile pyuria) help guide the diagnosis.
Live vaccines (MMR, varicella) should be deferred for 11 months after IVIG administration because passively acquired antibodies may interfere with vaccine immunogenicity.
Periungual desquamation of the fingers and toes (peeling) typically occurs 2-3 weeks after fever onset and is a late finding that can confirm the diagnosis retrospectively.
sources: AHA Scientific Statement: Diagnosis, Treatment, and Long-Term Management of Kawasaki Disease (2017) — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=AHA+Scientific+Statement%3A+Diagnosis%2C+Treatment%2C+and+Long-Term+Management+of+Kawasaki+Disease+%282017%29
UpToDate: Kawasaki disease: Clinical features and diagnosis — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search?search=Kawasaki+disease%3A+Clinical+features+and+diagnosis
UpToDate: Kawasaki disease: Initial treatment and prognosis — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search?search=Kawasaki+disease%3A+Initial+treatment+and+prognosis

after_stage2_more_likely: Kawasaki disease (5 days of fever + conjunctival injection + oral changes + rash + extremity changes + lymphadenopathy — meets all classic criteria)
Scarlet fever (strawberry tongue, rash — but typically has exudative pharyngitis)
after_stage2_less_likely: Measles (no Koplik spots, no cough/coryza/conjunctivitis prodrome in typical sequence, immunized)
Stevens-Johnson syndrome (no target lesions, no mucous membrane erosions, no drug exposure)
Toxic shock syndrome (not hypotensive, no desquamation pattern typical of TSS)
